note: Single flip() call, just demonstrates the primitive
viz(repeat(1000, flip)) — visualization of flip distribution, no computable answer
flip()+flip()+flip() — arithmetic on booleans demo
sumFlips thunk visualized with viz(repeat(100, sumFlips))

prompt: Define a function noisyDouble that takes a number x and, with 50% probability, returns x+x, and otherwise returns x unchanged. Apply it to 3.
groundtruth_code:
var noisyDouble = function(x) { return flip() ? x+x : x };
var ANSWER = noisyDouble(3);
selection_rationale: Tests basic stochastic function definition with conditional return values.

note: fairCoin repeated 20 times, visualized
trickCoin repeated 20 times, visualized
makeCoin factory with three coins visualized
bend higher-order function with viz(repeat(100, bentCoin))
weighted coin, viz of sum(repeat(10, coin)) distribution

prompt: Model a simple medical scenario where lung cancer occurs with probability 0.01 and a cold occurs with probability 0.2, independently. A patient coughs if they have a cold or lung cancer. Sample whether the patient coughs.
selection_rationale: Demonstrates basic causal generative model with noisy-OR structure.

prompt: Model a medical scenario with four diseases (lungCancer, TB, stomachFlu, cold, other) and four noisy-OR symptoms (cough, fever, chestPain, shortnessOfBreath). Return a record of the four symptom values.
selection_rationale: Tests multi-disease multi-symptom noisy-OR generative model returning a structured record.

prompt: Sample two independent fair coin flips and return them as a list.
groundtruth_code: var ANSWER = [flip(), flip()];
selection_rationale: Minimal test of joint sampling and list construction.

note: viz.hist of repeat(1000, randomPair) — histogram visualization only

prompt: Create a Bernoulli distribution with p=0.5, sample from it, and compute the log-probability of sampling true.
selection_rationale: Tests explicit distribution object creation, sampling, and log-probability scoring.

prompt: Create a Gaussian distribution with mean 0 and standard deviation 1, sample from it, and also draw a sample using the gaussian(0,1) helper. Compute one sample from the product of two independent standard Gaussian draws.
groundtruth_code:
var foo = function() { return gaussian(0,1) * gaussian(0,1) };
var ANSWER = foo();
selection_rationale: Tests Gaussian distribution construction and sampling helper usage.

prompt: Define a function that returns the product of two independent standard Gaussian samples. Use Infer with forward sampling (1000 samples) to construct its marginal distribution.
groundtruth_code:
var foo = function() { return gaussian(0, 1) * gaussian(0, 1); };
var ANSWER = Infer({method: 'forward', samples: 1000}, foo);
selection_rationale: Tests Infer with forward sampling to reify the marginal distribution of a compound stochastic process.

note: var A=flip(); var B=flip(); C=[A,B] — product rule illustration, no task

prompt: Model two flips A and B where B's probability depends on A: if A is true B has probability 0.3, otherwise 0.7. Use Infer with forward sampling (1000 samples) to get the joint marginal distribution over {A, B}.
selection_rationale: Tests conditional probability / product rule with dependent flips using forward inference.

note: flip() || flip() — sum rule illustration, single expression

prompt: Define a geometric distribution using stochastic recursion: with probability p return 0, otherwise return 1 plus a recursive call. Use Infer with forward sampling (1000 samples) on geometric(0.6) to obtain its distribution.
selection_rationale: Tests stochastic recursion and geometric distribution via forward inference.

note: eyeColor without mem — demonstrates broken semantics, not a task

prompt: Evaluate whether two independent calls to flip() return the same value.
groundtruth_code: var ANSWER = flip() == flip();
selection_rationale: Tests that two independent flip() calls can differ, illustrating non-memoized randomness.

prompt: Create a memoized version of flip using mem, then check whether two calls to the memoized flip return the same value.
groundtruth_code:
var memFlip = mem(flip);
var ANSWER = memFlip() == memFlip();
selection_rationale: Tests mem for persistent randomness: memoized flip always returns the same value.

prompt: Define a memoized eye-color function that assigns each person a persistent random eye color drawn uniformly from blue, green, and brown. Query bob twice and alice once, returning all three results as a list.
selection_rationale: Tests random-world style modeling with mem ensuring consistent per-entity random values.

prompt: Define a memoized function flipAlot that maps integers to persistent coin flips. Return two rows querying indices 1, 12, 47, and 1548, showing that the same index always returns the same flip.
selection_rationale: Tests that mem provides persistent randomness across multiple queries to the same key.

note: physics.animate falling objects — requires box2d/physics plugin
physics.animate block tower — requires box2d/physics plugin
physics.run stability inference — requires box2d/physics plugin
model returning D with viz — no conditioning, just illustrates prior distribution visually

prompt: Model three independent fair coin flips A, B, C. Their sum D = A+B+C. Given that D equals 3, infer the marginal distribution over A.
selection_rationale: Classic conditioning example showing that D==3 forces all flips to be true.

prompt: Model three independent fair coin flips A, B, C with D = A+B+C. Given that D is at least 2, infer the marginal distribution over A.
selection_rationale: Tests conditioning on an inequality, yielding a non-degenerate posterior over A.

prompt: Implement rejection sampling manually: flip A, B, C, compute D=A+B+C, and if D>=2 return A, otherwise recurse. Draw 100 samples and collect them.
selection_rationale: Illustrates manual rejection sampling as the basis for conditional inference.

prompt: Model three independent fair coin flips A, B, C with D=A+B+C. Condition on D>=2 and use rejection sampling with 100 samples to infer the distribution over A.
selection_rationale: Tests rejection sampling method in Infer for discrete conditioning.

prompt: Model three independent fair coin flips A, B, C with D=A+B+C. Condition on D>=2 and use enumeration to compute the exact distribution over A.
selection_rationale: Tests exact enumeration inference for a simple discrete conditioning problem.

prompt: Implement Bayesian inference with a prior (fair flip for hypothesis), a likelihood (flip with p=0.9 if hypothesis true, p=0.1 otherwise), and condition on observedData=true. Return the posterior distribution over hypothesis.
selection_rationale: Demonstrates the prior/likelihood/posterior decomposition of Bayes rule.

note: ~~~-fenced Gaussian rejection sampling block — infinite loop, never terminates, intentionally shown as broken

prompt: Model a latent true value trueX drawn from a standard Gaussian. Observe a noisy measurement of 0.2 via a Gaussian with mean trueX and standard deviation 0.1. Use rejection sampling with maxScore=2 and 1000 samples to infer the posterior over trueX.
selection_rationale: Tests the observe operator for continuous conditioning, recovering a posterior near the observed value.

prompt: Model three independent fair coin flips A, B, C and condition on their sum A+B+C being at least 2. Return the marginal distribution over A using Infer.
selection_rationale: Tests conditioning on a complex proposition not appearing as a named variable in the model.

note: condition(A) with viz — trivial, shows condition forces A=true, no inference task

prompt: Use a factor statement to softly prefer heads: flip A, apply factor(A?1:0), and infer the resulting distribution over A.
selection_rationale: Demonstrates that factor adds to the log-probability of an execution, producing a soft bias toward true.

note: tug of war generative model printing results — forward sampling only, no inference task

prompt: Model a tug of war where strength is memoized and laziness is per-match. Condition on Bob and Mary beating Tom and Sue, and also Bob and Sue beating Tom and Jim. Use MCMC (MH, 25000 samples) to infer the posterior distribution over Bob's strength.
selection_rationale: Tests conditioning a continuous-valued causal model using MCMC, inferring strength from win observations.

prompt: In the tug of war model, condition on Mary's strength being at least Sue's and on Bob beating Jim one-on-one. Infer the probability that the team of Bob and Mary beats Jim and Sue using MCMC (MH, 25000 samples).
selection_rationale: Tests complex multi-condition inference predicting a future event in the tug of war model.

note: physics.animate block drop — requires box2d/physics plugin
physics.run inverse inference with editor.MCMCProgress — requires physics plugin and editor API

prompt: Model breast cancer diagnosis: cancer occurs with probability 0.01. A mammogram is positive with probability 0.8 if cancer is present, and 0.096 otherwise. Given a positive mammogram, infer the distribution over whether cancer is present using enumeration.
selection_rationale: Classic Bayesian medical diagnosis illustrating base-rate neglect and correct posterior computation.

prompt: Model a causal mammography scenario: breast cancer occurs with probability 0.01 and a benign cyst with probability 0.2. A positive mammogram results from cancer (p=0.8) or a cyst (p=0.5). Given a positive mammogram, infer the distribution over breast cancer using enumeration.
selection_rationale: Tests a more intuitive causal framing of the mammography problem with an explicit alternative cause.

prompt: Model five diseases (lungCancer, TB, cold, stomachFlu, other) causing four symptoms (cough, fever, chestPain, shortnessOfBreath) via noisy-OR. Given that all four symptoms are present, infer the joint distribution over lungCancer and TB using enumeration.
selection_rationale: Tests enumeration inference on a multi-disease multi-symptom noisy-OR network with all symptoms observed.

prompt: Extend the medical model with demographic factors: worksInHospital (p=0.01) and smokes (p=0.2) contribute causally to TB, lungCancer, and cold. Given cough, chestPain, and shortnessOfBreath, infer the joint distribution over lungCancer and TB using enumeration.
selection_rationale: Tests extended causal network with demographic risk factors, showing how knowledge can grow without restructuring.

note: C=flip(); B=flip(); A=B?flip(0.1):flip(0.4) — illustrates causal dependence, no inference task

prompt: Model a medical diagnosis scenario with smokes, lungDisease, cold, cough, fever, chestPain, and shortnessOfBreath. Given that the patient coughs, use enumeration to infer the marginal distributions over cold and lungDisease.
selection_rationale: Tests Bayesian inference of disease causes given symptom observation using enumeration.

note: Noisy-AND with context-dependent evaluation of B — illustrates structural causal dependence only

prompt: Implement the do-operator (intervention) on a causal model where A is set to a fixed value (not conditioned). Define BdoA(Aval) that sets A directly and returns the distribution over B where B=flip(0.1) if A else flip(0.4). Return results for both Aval=true and Aval=false.
selection_rationale: Demonstrates the do-operator (graph surgery) as distinct from conditioning for detecting causal dependence.

prompt: Model the medical diagnosis network (smokes, lungDisease, cold, cough, fever, chestPain, shortnessOfBreath) without any conditioning. Use enumeration to get the joint prior distribution over cough and cold.
selection_rationale: Computes the prior joint distribution to establish a baseline before interventions.

prompt: Apply the do-operator to set cold=true in the medical model (intervention, not conditioning). Use enumeration to get the distribution over cough and cold after intervention.
selection_rationale: Tests do-operator intervention vs. observation: forcing cold=true increases cough probability.

prompt: Test statistical dependence: define BcondA(Aval) that conditions on A==Aval in a model where B=flip(0.1) if A else flip(0.4) and C is an independent flip. Return the distribution over B for both Aval=true and Aval=false.
selection_rationale: Tests statistical dependence detection via conditioning, showing A and B are correlated.

prompt: Demonstrate statistical dependence from a common cause: C is a fair flip. A depends on C (flip(0.5) or flip(0.9)). B depends on C (flip(0.1) or flip(0.4)). Return the distribution over B given A=true and A=false.
selection_rationale: Illustrates that statistical dependence can arise from a common cause even without direct causal links.

prompt: Demonstrate screening off: C is a fair flip, B depends on C (flip(0.5) or flip(0.9)), A depends on C (flip(0.1) or flip(0.4)). Condition on C being true and on A==Aval. Show that B is independent of A given C by returning B's distribution for both Aval=true and Aval=false.
selection_rationale: Tests screening off: B and A become independent once their common cause C is observed.

prompt: Demonstrate explaining away: A and B are independent fair flips. C is their noisy-OR effect (C=true with p=0.9 if A or B, p=0.2 otherwise). Condition on C being true. Return B's distribution given A=true and A=false.
selection_rationale: Tests explaining away: observing a common effect C creates anti-correlation between its independent causes A and B.

note: ~~~~norun schematic of explaining away template — pseudo-code, not runnable

prompt: Sample A and B as independent uniform integers from 0 to 9. Condition on A+B==9. Return the joint posterior distribution over {A, B}.
selection_rationale: Tests explaining-away anti-correlation: conditioning on a fixed sum creates perfect anti-correlation between two independent variables.

prompt: Sample A and B as independent uniform integers from 0 to 9. Condition on A==B. Return the joint posterior distribution over {A, B}.
selection_rationale: Tests conditional dependence: conditioning on equality induces perfect positive correlation between independent variables.

prompt: In the medical model (smokes, lungDisease, cold, cough, fever, chestPain, shortnessOfBreath), condition on cough, chestPain, and shortnessOfBreath all being true. Infer the posterior distribution over smokes using enumeration.
selection_rationale: Tests that multiple downstream symptoms raise the probability of smoking via the causal chain through lungDisease.

prompt: In the medical model, condition on both lungDisease being true and on cough, chestPain, and shortnessOfBreath all being true. Infer the posterior over smokes to demonstrate screening off by the intermediate variable.
selection_rationale: Tests screening off: once lungDisease is known, the downstream symptoms provide no additional evidence about smokes.

prompt: In the medical model, condition only on cough being true. Infer the joint marginal distribution over cold and lungDisease to see explaining away.
selection_rationale: Tests that observing cough raises both cold and lungDisease from their base rates through explaining away.

prompt: In the medical model, condition on cough being true and cold being false. Infer the joint posterior over cold and lungDisease to see that ruling out cold strongly raises lungDisease.
selection_rationale: Tests explaining away: ruling out cold as a cause of cough dramatically increases the posterior on lungDisease.

prompt: In the medical model, condition on cough and cold both being true. Infer the joint posterior over cold and lungDisease to confirm that having cold explains away cough, returning lungDisease to near its prior.
selection_rationale: Tests explaining away: cold explains the cough, so lungDisease returns toward its base rate.

prompt: Model student performance: examFair (p=0.8) and doesHomework (p=0.8) jointly determine passing an exam. Condition on not passing. Infer the joint posterior over doesHomework and examFair using enumeration.
selection_rationale: Tests attribution inference: failing an exam raises probability that either the student didn't study or the exam was unfair.

prompt: Extend the student performance model to multiple students and exams using memoized functions. Condition on Bill failing exam1. Infer the joint posterior over doesHomework for Bill and examFair for exam1.
selection_rationale: Tests memoized multi-student multi-exam attribution model using Bayesian inference.

prompt: Model the blicket detector: each block has a persistent blicket property (p=0.4). A blicket block powers the machine with p=0.9; non-blicket blocks with p=0.05. The machine activates if any block activates it. Condition on the machine activating with blocks A and B together. Return the posterior probability that A is a blicket.
selection_rationale: Tests the blicket detector causal learning model illustrating explaining away from common effect observation.

prompt: Model reflectance-illumination perception: reflectance ~ Gaussian(1,1), illumination ~ Gaussian(3,1), luminance = reflectance*illumination. Observe luminance=3 via a noisy Gaussian (sigma=1). Use MCMC (10000 samples) to infer the posterior over reflectance and return its expected value.
selection_rationale: Tests MCMC inference for a continuous non-linear perception model (checker shadow illusion).

prompt: Extend the reflectance model by also observing that illumination is approximately 0.5 (via Gaussian with sigma=0.1), representing a shadow. Use MCMC (10000 samples) to infer the posterior reflectance and return its expected value.
selection_rationale: Tests explaining away in perception: observing shadow evidence (low illumination) raises estimated reflectance.

note: makeBag with mem, viz(repeat(100,...)) — generative demonstration, no inference task

prompt: Model bags of marbles: each bag has a memoized Dirichlet-sampled color distribution over 5 colors. Observe the given bag/draw pairs and use MCMC (20000 samples) to infer the posterior predictive draw for bag1, bag2, bag3, and a new unseen bagN.
selection_rationale: Tests hierarchical Bayesian learning of per-bag color distributions with posterior predictive inference.

prompt: Add a global prototype to the bag model: sample phi from a Dirichlet, scale to get prototype = phi*numColors, and use this as the prior for each bag's Dirichlet. Use MCMC (20000 samples) to infer posterior predictives for bag1, bag2, bag3, and bagN given the same observed data.
selection_rationale: Tests hierarchical model with global prototype enabling transfer learning across bags.

note: learning curves (viz.line) comparing MSE at specific vs global level — no clean ANSWER value
same learning curves from single bag — viz.line only

prompt: Model bags with a learnable regularity: phi is a global Dirichlet prototype, and alpha ~ Gamma(2,2) controls prototype strength. Use MCMC (30000 samples) with the uniform-per-bag observed data to infer posterior predictives for bag1 through bag4, bagN, and the alpha hyperparameter.
selection_rationale: Tests overhypothesis learning: alpha parameter captures within-bag regularity, enabling one-shot generalization to bag4.

prompt: Run the same overhypothesis model but with heterogeneous observed data (bags mix all colors). Use MCMC (30000 samples) to infer posterior predictives and the alpha hyperparameter.
selection_rationale: Tests that heterogeneous data leads to high alpha, weakening one-shot generalization for bag4.

prompt: Model the shape bias task: 4 object attributes, 11 possible values each. Each attribute has a per-attribute prototype (phi*alpha). Observe training data where cat1-cat4 have consistent shapes but varying colors/textures. Use MCMC (10000 samples) to infer posterior predictive shape and color for cat5 (seen once) and catN (novel).
selection_rationale: Tests hierarchical overhypothesis model for the shape bias: shape is homogeneous within categories, enabling one-shot generalization.

prompt: Model X-bar phrase structure headedness: a global languageDir bias ~ Beta(1,1) determines per-head directionality. Observe one data point [D, N] and use MCMC (20000 samples) to infer whether N appears second or first.
selection_rationale: Tests hierarchical model for linguistic parameter transfer: X-bar headedness learned across phrase types.

note: Timing rejection sampling with time() — benchmarking demo, no inferential result
Timing enumeration with time() — benchmarking demo
Timing enumeration with flexible numFlips — benchmarking demo
Timing MCMC with time() — benchmarking demo
rejection viz(Infer({rejection})) — same model as block 1-3, viz only
enumerate viz(Infer({enumerate})) — duplicate of conditioning chapter, viz only
MCMC viz(Infer({MCMC})) — duplicate with viz only
Markov chain states a,b,c,d with viz.hist — convergence visualization
Uniform transition chain — illustrates faster convergence, viz.hist only

prompt: Define a Markov chain over integers where p=0.7. From state 3, the chain can stay or go to 4. From other states, the chain goes left (p=0.5), stays (p=0.5*(1-p)), or goes right (p=0.5*(1-p)). Run the chain from state 3 for 250 steps, repeating 5000 times. Collect the samples.
selection_rationale: Tests a Markov chain over integers that converges to a geometric distribution conditioned to be >= 3.

prompt: Use MCMC (25000 samples, lag 10) to sample from a geometric distribution (flip(p)==true returns 1, otherwise 1+recurse) conditioned on the result being greater than 2, with p=0.7.
selection_rationale: Tests MCMC inference on a conditioned geometric distribution, comparing to a manually-constructed Markov chain.

prompt: Implement the Metropolis-Hastings algorithm manually for a geometric distribution conditioned on x>=3 with p=0.7. Use a symmetric proposal (x+1 or x-1). Run the chain for 10000 iterations starting from state 3. Return the chain of samples.
selection_rationale: Tests full manual implementation of the MH recipe for a conditioned geometric distribution.

note: Duplicate of hand-crafted transition function — just restates the function, no new task
constrainedSumModel with MCMC_Callbacks.finalAccept — requires callbacks plugin
constrainedSumModel with HMC + MCMC_Callbacks — requires callbacks plugin
pairwiseSameModel with MCMC — viz of marginalized distribution only

prompt: Model five uniform(0,1) random variables a-e where consecutive pairs are constrained to be approximately equal (via Gaussian observe with sigma=0.005). Interleave the observations with sampling. Use SMC with 1000 particles to infer the posterior. Return 6 samples.
selection_rationale: Tests SMC inference on a model with interleaved observations, showing particle filtering advantage over MCMC.

note: trajectory generation with Draw canvas plugin
trajectory inference with Draw canvas + SMC — requires Draw plugin
~~~~.norun Gaussian({mu: param(), sigma: 0.1}) — param() syntax demonstration
gaussianModel with guide annotations but no data — incomplete template
Target model + guide family shown separately — no data variable defined, not runnable

prompt: Generate 100 samples from a Gaussian with true mean 3.5 and true sigma 0.8. Infer the posterior over mu and sigma using variational inference (optimize method, adam with stepSize=0.25, 250 steps, 1000 posterior samples). Use guide functions with param() for mu and sigma.
selection_rationale: Tests variational inference (VI) for learning Gaussian parameters, contrasting with MCMC.

prompt: Same Gaussian parameter estimation as above but using the default mean-field guide (no explicit guide functions). Infer mu and sigma using optimize with adam (stepSize=0.25), 250 steps, 1000 samples.
selection_rationale: Tests default mean-field VI without explicit guide specification.

prompt: Model reflectance and illumination (both Gaussian priors) with luminance = reflectance*illumination. Observe luminance=3 with Gaussian noise (sigma=1). Use MCMC (15000 samples, lag 100) to infer the joint posterior over reflectance and illumination.
selection_rationale: Tests MCMC on the reflectance-illumination model to reveal posterior correlation between variables.

note: Mean-field explicit guide reflectance model — viz.heatMap only, no clean ANSWER

prompt: Model reflectance-illumination with a correlated variational guide: the illumination guide mean depends linearly on reflectance via params. Use optimize (adam stepSize=0.01, 10000 steps, 1000 samples) to capture posterior correlation.
selection_rationale: Tests structured variational inference with a correlated guide capturing the posterior anti-correlation.

prompt: Define a coin-flipping model with a continuous prior on coin weight (uniform 0–1). Observe 5 heads in a row. Infer the posterior distribution over coin weight.
selection_rationale: Canonical Bayesian coin-weight posterior update after binary observations.

note: learning curve viz using viz.line over data sizes
viz.marginals of sequence conditional distribution
sequenceCondDist with viz.marginals
i.i.d. word sequence generative model, no inference
non-i.i.d. word sequence generative model, no inference

prompt: Model a bag of marbles where each marble is drawn i.i.d. from an unknown categorical distribution. Condition on observing the sequence ['b','b','b','b','b']. Infer the posterior predictive distribution over the next marble color from the set ['a','b','c','d'].
selection_rationale: Demonstrates posterior predictive inference over exchangeable sequences with Dirichlet prior.

note: ~~~~norun de Finetti exchangeability template

prompt: Implement a Polya urn model: start with one red and one blue ball. Sample 10 times, each time adding the drawn ball back plus one more of the same color. Infer the distribution of the final urn state (count of reds vs blues) after 10 draws.
selection_rationale: Polya urn as a concrete generative process for Dirichlet-process-like exchangeable sequences.

prompt: Implement a de Finetti urn model: draw a weight w from Uniform(0,1), then use that fixed weight for all subsequent coin flips. Infer the distribution over the 11th flip given 10 heads in a row.
selection_rationale: de Finetti representation links exchangeability to latent parameter inference.

note: ~~~~norun ideal learner template skeleton

prompt: Define a model with a structured prior over coin fairness: the coin is fair (weight 0.5) with probability 0.999, otherwise its weight is drawn from Beta(1,1). Condition on observing 5 heads in a row, and infer the posterior distribution over the binary 'isFair' variable.
selection_rationale: Structured hypothesis space with spike-and-slab prior captures discrete model selection over coin fairness.

prompt: Infer the posterior distribution over coin weight using a structured prior: the coin is fair (weight=0.5) with probability 0.999, otherwise weight ~ Beta(1,1). Observe 15 heads out of 20 flips using a Binomial likelihood and return the posterior over weight.
selection_rationale: Demonstrates spike-and-slab prior for Bayesian model selection in coin weight estimation.

note: learning curve viz.line over data sizes for coin weight
viz.line learning curve for beta pseudoCounts structured prior
viz.line learning curve for structured hypothesis space

prompt: Model elemental causal induction with a noisy-or interaction: both a cause C and background noise b can produce effect E. Given observations [{C:true,E:true},{C:true,E:true},{C:false,E:false},{C:true,E:true}], infer the posterior distribution over causal power (cp) of C.
selection_rationale: Elemental causal induction via noisy-or: parameter learning for causal power from contingency data.

prompt: Model a mixture of 3 bags of marbles with a shared Dirichlet prototype prior (alpha=0.1). Assign 6 observed marbles to bags using MCMC. Infer whether obs1 and obs2 came from the same bag and whether obs1 and obs3 came from the same bag.
selection_rationale: Basic finite mixture model with latent bag assignments and Dirichlet prototype prior.

prompt: Extend the marble mixture model from block 0 by adding a learned mixture distribution over bags (drawn from Dirichlet). Assign 6 named observations to 3 bags using MCMC, and infer whether obs1/obs2 and obs1/obs3 came from the same bag.
selection_rationale: Mixture distribution variant adds learned bag weights, illustrating full mixture model formulation.

prompt: Fit a 2-component Gaussian mixture model to 20 2D observations (two clusters near (1,1) and (-1,-1)). For each observation, assign it to one of two latent categories with shared Dirichlet mixture weights. Infer the posterior predictive mean {xMean, yMean} for a new observation 'newObs'.
selection_rationale: Gaussian mixture model discovering two spatial clusters from unlabeled 2D data.

prompt: Model categorical perception: two Gaussian vowel categories (prototype1=0, prototype2=5) with equal mixing. For each stimulus value in range(0, 5, 0.2), compute the expected perceived value after observing the stimulus through a noisy channel (Gaussian noise sigma=1). Return the expected perceived value for stimulus 2.5.
selection_rationale: Categorical perception as Bayesian inference: shrinkage toward category prototypes demonstrates perceptual magnet effect.

prompt: Implement LDA topic modeling on a 6-document corpus about biology ('DNA evolution') and linguistics ('parsing phonology') with 2 topics and 4 vocabulary words. Infer the expected word probability for each topic using MCMC.
selection_rationale: LDA as a probabilistic program demonstrates learning topic-word distributions from co-occurrence patterns.

prompt: Model an unknown number of coins: either 1 or 2 coins generated the 8 observed outcomes [T,T,T,T,F,F,F,F]. Using rejection sampling, infer the posterior distribution over the number of coins.
selection_rationale: Unbounded mixture model inferring number of latent categories from binary sequence.

prompt: Model a mixture of an unknown number of marble bags using a Poisson(1) prior on bag count. Observe 6 marbles ['red','red','blue','blue','red','blue']. Infer the posterior distribution over the number of bags.
selection_rationale: Unbounded Dirichlet-multinomial mixture with Poisson prior on category count for marble categorization.

note: residuals function demo — computes residual probs, no probabilistic inference
mySampleDiscrete viz demo — no meaningful inference task
Dirichlet + mySampleDiscrete viz demo
beta residuals infinite sampler demo — no inference

prompt: Construct an infinite mixture model of 6 named marble observations using a DP prior (beta residuals with mem). Observe colors ['red','blue','red','blue','red','blue']. Infer whether obs1/obs2 and obs1/obs3 came from the same infinite-mixture bag.
selection_rationale: Infinite mixture model using DP stick-breaking for unbounded category inference with MCMC.

prompt: Model a Binomial election poll: observe k=1 out of n=20 voters support candidate A, with a uniform prior on the true proportion p. Return the joint posterior over {prior, priorPredictive, posterior, posteriorPredictive}.
selection_rationale: Canonical BDA example illustrating prior, posterior, and predictive distributions for binomial proportion.

prompt: Compute the posterior expected value of the election proportion p (uniform prior, k=1 out of n=20 successes, Binomial likelihood).
selection_rationale: Demonstrates using expectation() to summarize a posterior — the mean of the inferred proportion.

prompt: Compute the probability that the election proportion p lies in the interval [0.01, 0.18] given uniform prior, k=1, n=20.
selection_rationale: Credible interval calculation demonstrating how to compute posterior probabilities over intervals.

prompt: Implement the findHDI function that finds the highest density interval (HDI) for a distribution. Test it on a censored Gaussian (x>0 condition): find the 95% HDI starting with interval [-10, 10] and step size 0.1.
selection_rationale: HDI computation algorithm illustrates principled Bayesian credible interval finding via greedy shrinkage.

prompt: Fit a logistic regression model to voting data by age: {age:[20,30,40,50], n:20 each, k:[1,5,17,18]}. Use Gaussian priors on slope a and intercept b. Infer the posterior over {a, b}.
selection_rationale: Bayesian logistic regression for binary outcome data with Gaussian priors on regression coefficients.

prompt: Extend the logistic regression from block 4 to produce posterior predictive counts for each age group. Infer the posterior and compute expected predictive count at each age; return the vector of expected predictive counts.
selection_rationale: Posterior predictive check computes expected counts from logistic regression for model checking.

prompt: Model selection between a 'simple' model (p=0.5) and a 'complex' model (p ~ Uniform(0,1)) for k=5 successes out of n=20. Use a flip(0.5) prior on models. Infer the posterior distribution over which model is preferred.
selection_rationale: Bayesian model selection with Occam's razor penalizing the flexible model via marginal likelihood.

prompt: Compute the Bayes Factor between a simple model (Binomial p=0.5) and a complex model (p ~ Uniform(0,1)) for k=5, n=20. Return the ratio of their marginal likelihoods (simpleLikelihood / complexLikelihood).
selection_rationale: Bayes Factor computation directly from marginal likelihoods for nested model comparison.

prompt: Compute the Savage-Dickey density ratio for k=5, n=20: estimate the prior and posterior density of p near 0.5 (interval [0.45, 0.55]), and return their ratio as a Bayes Factor approximation.
selection_rationale: Savage-Dickey density ratio: an elegant shortcut for Bayes Factors in nested model comparison.

prompt: Model a widget factory: a widget machine produces widgets sampled from Categorical({vs:[.2,.3,.4,.5,.6,.7,.8], ps:[.05,.1,.2,.3,.2,.1,.05]}). A tester accepts widgets above a tolerance threshold by rejection. Observe widgets [.6,.7,.8] and infer the tolerance using direct condition (no nested Infer).
selection_rationale: Introduces the widget-tester prelude as foundation for nested inference / social cognition modeling.

prompt: Rewrite the widget-tester inference using nested Infer: getGoodWidget returns sample(Infer({condition: widget>tolerance})). Observe [.6,.7,.8] and infer tolerance.
selection_rationale: Nested Infer version of widget tester shows how inner inference abstracts agent reasoning.

prompt: Widget tester with observe: getGoodWidget returns the full Infer distribution, and the outer query uses observe() rather than condition(). Infer tolerance from actualWidgets [.6,.7,.8].
selection_rationale: Observe-based nested inference shows the outer-Infer-observe-inner-Infer pattern for reasoning about reasoning.

note: ~~~~norun chooseAction template with ... placeholder for Infer method
~~~~norun specialized chooseAction with undefined actionPrior

prompt: Sally wants a cookie from a vending machine with two buttons: button 'a' gives bagel, button 'b' gives cookie (deterministic). Using an action prior of equal probability over ['a','b'], infer the distribution over Sally's action.
selection_rationale: Rational action selection via goal-conditioned Infer: action distribution given goal state.

prompt: Stochastic vending machine: button 'a' gives bagel with p=0.9/cookie with p=0.1, button 'b' gives bagel with p=0.1/cookie with p=0.9. Infer Sally's action distribution if her goal is 'cookie'.
selection_rationale: Stochastic vending machine produces soft-max optimal policy: action probability proportional to goal satisfaction.

prompt: Inverse planning: stochastic vending machine (same as block 6). Sally presses button 'b'. Infer the posterior distribution over her goal (bagel or cookie).
selection_rationale: Inverse planning: outer Infer inverts inner chooseAction to infer goals from observed actions.

prompt: Broken button vending machine: button 'a' gives bagel p=0.9, button 'b' gives bagel/cookie equally (p=0.5/0.5). Sally presses 'b'. Infer her goal (bagel or cookie). Why is cookie still preferred despite button b being broken?
selection_rationale: Demonstrates counterfactual reasoning in goal inference: cookie preferred because pressing 'a' was the better bagel option.

prompt: Infer Sally's preference (probability of wanting bagel vs cookie) given she presses button 'b' three times. Use MCMC with a continuous uniform preference prior; return the distribution over her next goal.
selection_rationale: Hierarchical preference inference: continuous latent preference inferred from multiple action observations.

prompt: Symmetric vending machine (both buttons give cookie p=0.9): observe Sally pressing 'b' three times. Infer the distribution over her next goal given uniform preference prior. How does this compare to the asymmetric machine case?
selection_rationale: Symmetric alternative actions eliminate goal inference signal, demonstrating principle of efficiency.

prompt: Observer infers the vending machine's button mappings from Sally's actions: machine params are unknown (uniform per button), Sally's goal is 'cookie', observer conditions on Sally's goal=cookie and observes action='b'. Infer machine behavior at buttons a and b.
selection_rationale: Inferring the world from actions: observer learns machine structure by conditioning on rationality principle.

prompt: Multi-press vending machine (actions ['a','aa','aaa'] with probs [0.7,0.2,0.1]). Machine mapping unknown (uniform per action). Sally's goal is cookie. Observer conditions on Sally pressing 'a' once. Infer machine behavior at one-press and two-press actions.
selection_rationale: Principle of efficiency: pressing once vs twice reveals different information about machine outcomes.

prompt: Joint inference about goal and machine knowledge: Sally presses 'aa' (two presses) and we observe the outcome is cookie. Infer Sally's goal, the probability that one press gives cookie, and the probability that two presses give cookie.
selection_rationale: Joint inference over goal and machine structure from action+outcome reveals U-shaped one-press distribution.

prompt: Infer whether Sally is knowledgeable about the true vending machine (button a → bagel 90%, button b → cookie 90%) given that she announces goal 'cookie' but presses button 'a'. Return the posterior over her knowledge state.
selection_rationale: Inferring knowledge state: pressing wrong button suggests ignorance about true machine mappings.

prompt: Infer Sally's belief about button a (how likely it gives bagel) given she has goal='cookie' but presses button 'a', while the true machine gives bagel 90% from button a. Return posterior over Sally's belief about button a.
selection_rationale: False belief inference: observer infers Sally believes button a gives cookie, explaining her suboptimal action.

note: ~~~~norun teacher/learner recursion template with ... placeholder
~~~~norun learner template with undefined roll()
~~~~norun teacher/learner with depth — undefined roll() function

prompt: Implement a pedagogical (teacher-learner) model for two dice A and B with three sides (red,green,blue) where die A has probs [0,.2,.8] and die B has probs [.1,.3,.6]. Use depth-3 recursion. Infer the learner's posterior distribution over which die the teacher intended when the teacher shows the green side.
selection_rationale: Pedagogical model of natural pedagogy: mutual recursion between teacher and learner enables non-obvious inference.

note: ~~~~norun speaker/listener RSA template with ... placeholder
~~~~norun listener with literalProb — no runnable inference

prompt: Implement a scalar implicature RSA model: 3 plants with states 0–3, utterances ['all','some','none'], literal meanings (all→state==3, some→state>0, none→state==0). Use depth-1 recursion. Infer listener's posterior over state after hearing 'some'.
selection_rationale: RSA scalar implicature: 'some but not all' emerges from recursive pragmatic reasoning at depth 1.

prompt: Size principle model: 'Big' hypothesis has 6 elements {a-f}, 'Small' has 3 elements {a-c}. Equal prior over hypotheses. Observe data=['a']. Infer the posterior over which hypothesis generated the data.
selection_rationale: Size principle: smaller consistent hypothesis preferred after single observation via Bayesian Occam's razor.

note: viz.line learning trajectory for Big/Small hypothesis over data sizes
Rectangle game uses Draw plugin (img.rectangle, img.circle) for visualization
~~~~norun observedData variants for rectangle game
Weak sampling rectangle game also uses Draw plugin

prompt: Bayesian Occam's razor: hypothesis A is skewed toward {a,b} (probs [.375,.375,.125,.125]) while hypothesis B is uniform over {a,b,c,d}. Observe data=['a','b','a','b','c','d','b','b']. Infer the posterior over which hypothesis generated the data.
selection_rationale: General Bayesian Occam's razor: skewed hypothesis preferred when data matches its high-probability region.

prompt: Fair/unfair coin model selection: fair coin (weight=0.5) has prior 0.999, unfair coin weight ~ Beta(1,1). Observe ['h','h','t','h','t','h','h','h','t','h']. Infer the posterior over {prior, fair, weight}.
selection_rationale: Fair/unfair coin model selection demonstrates Bayesian Occam's razor penalizing flexible hypothesis.

note: viz.line learning trajectory for fair/unfair coin using optimize

prompt: Unused parameter test: fair/unfair model where unfairWeight is always drawn from Beta(1,1) even when fair=true. Observe ['h']. Show the posterior still correctly favors fairness despite equal parameter count.
selection_rationale: Unused parameters don't increase model complexity — only flexibility that affects predictions is penalized.

prompt: Polynomial curve fitting with Bayesian Occam's razor: fit a polynomial of unknown order (0–3) to 9 noisy observations. Use optimize with 4 Gaussian(0,2) coefficients and a uniform prior over order. Infer the posterior over {order, coeff0, coeff1, coeff2, coeff3}.
selection_rationale: Polynomial curve fitting with model order selection via Bayesian Occam's razor.

note: Data generation snippet: samples polynomial + Gaussian noise, no inference

prompt: Scene inference: infer whether an observed 1x2 red patch is one 1x2 block or two 1x1 blocks. Use a 4x2 grid, layered rendering, and a 50/50 prior on numObjects. Return the posterior distribution over numObjects.
selection_rationale: Perceptual scene inference via Bayesian Occam's razor: one-object hypothesis slightly preferred over two.

prompt: Motion-based scene inference: two frames show a 1x2 red patch moving from x=1 to x=2. Objects move via a random drift model (left/right/stay with probs [0.3,0.4,0.3]). Infer whether 1 or 2 objects produced the motion.
selection_rationale: Common fate (motion coherence) strongly favors one-object interpretation via Occam's razor on coincidences.

prompt: Generate a random arithmetic expression using stochastic recursion: primitives are integers 0–9 and binary operators {+,-,*,/,**}. Return a single sampled expression string.
selection_rationale: Stochastic recursion generates infinite arithmetic expression language with implicit complexity prior.

note: Infer enumerate maxExecutions:100 + viz.table of randomArithmeticExpression

prompt: Induce an arithmetic function of variable x from examples. Generate expressions combining {0-9, x, +, -, *, /, **} via stochastic recursion. Enumerate the top-100 expressions consistent with f(1)==3. Return the distribution over expression strings.
selection_rationale: Language-of-thought arithmetic induction: infinite hypothesis space with simplicity prior from generative grammar.

prompt: Rational Rules model for concept learning: given 5 'fep' and 4 'non-fep' training stimuli with 4 binary traits, use a grammar over disjunctive normal form rules with tau=0.3 stopping probability and noise exp(-1.5). Enumerate 1000 rules and return posterior predictive classification probability for all 15 objects.
selection_rationale: Rational Rules model: grammar-based concept induction producing graded generalization from noisy data.

prompt: Sampling hypothesis: an agent observes a coin flipped 5 times (4 heads). A max agent always bets on the most likely next outcome. A sample agent draws a single sample from their belief distribution. Simulate 100 agents of each type and return the distribution of their decisions.
selection_rationale: Sampling hypothesis: probability matching behavior emerges from single-sample agents vs. maximizing agents.

note: residuals function demo — computes residual probabilities, no inference
mySampleDiscrete on fixed residuals with viz.auto — no inference

prompt: Implement makeSticks using mem and beta to create a stick-breaking Dirichlet Process sampler. Sample 1000 draws from mySticks = makeSticks(1) and return their distribution.
selection_rationale: Stick-breaking DP construction: lazy infinite discrete distribution from memoized beta draws.

prompt: Implement DPthunk wrapping a Gaussian base distribution with alpha=1. Sample 10000 draws from both a raw Gaussian and the DPthunk-memoized version. Return a sample from the memoized Gaussian.
selection_rationale: DPthunk construction shows how DP concentrates a continuous base distribution into a discrete one.

prompt: Implement DPmem as a higher-order function that stochastically memoizes any procedure using a per-argument DP. Apply it to geometric(p) and return a distribution over 10000 draws of memoizedGeometric(0.5).
selection_rationale: DPmem is a stochastic memoizer: repeated calls with same args return previously sampled values with high probability.

note: memoizedGaussian viz.marginals — no inference task
two-panel viz comparing Gaussian and memoizedGaussian

prompt: Infinite Dirichlet-multinomial mixture: 6 named marble observations with colors ['blue','green','red']. Use DPmem(1, uuid) as bag prior. Condition on color data via factor. Infer whether obs1/obs2 and obs1/obs3 came from the same bag.
selection_rationale: Infinite Dirichlet-multinomial mixture using DPmem(uuid) as bag prior for unbounded category inference.

note: reusableCategories viz demo — shows DPmem category reuse, no inference
Infinite Gaussian mixture generative model — samples but no inference

prompt: Goldwater Model 1: word segmentation. Use DPmem to learn reusable words as sequences of phones drawn from a Categorical over 14 phone types. Sample 10 words from the model using the learned DP. Return a sample utterance.
selection_rationale: Goldwater Model 1: DPmem creates reusable word forms, modeling Zipf's law in word frequency.

prompt: Infinite Hidden Markov Model: vocabulary = ['chef','omelet','soup','eat','work','bake']. States drawn from a global DPmem(uuid). Each state has its own DP transition model and Dirichlet observation model. Generate a sample word sequence starting from 'start'.
selection_rationale: Infinite HMM: DPmem creates unbounded state space with shared global state source via nested DPs.

prompt: Infinite Relational Model: infer social groups from who-talks-to-whom data. 5 people (tom, jim, fred, mary, sue, ann). Use DPmem(uuid) for class assignments and beta(0.5,0.5) talk probabilities per class pair. Infer whether tom/fred and tom/mary share the same class.
selection_rationale: Infinite Relational Model discovers latent social groups from relational talk-data.

note: CrossCat generative sample — TODO note in source, returns one observation, no inference
nCRP sample of (sub, top) category pairs — viz.table, no inference
nCRP generative model with observation — viz.table, no inference conditioning

prompt: Hierarchical Dirichlet Process: poisson(20) base measure, topLevel DPmem(10), two component DPs (component1, component2) each sharing topLevel as base. Return 1000 samples from each component and from topLevel.
selection_rationale: HDP: component DPs share vocabulary from top-level DP, modeling topic models and related problems.

note: nCRP + HDP combined model with viz — no quantitative inference task

prompt: Deterministic utility-maximizing agent choosing between 3 restaurants: donut (cost 1, utility 3), veg (cost 2, utility 5), noodle (cost 2, utility 4). The agent picks the option with highest utility minus cost. Return the agent's chosen restaurant.
selection_rationale: Deterministic expected-utility maximizing agent for single-step decision problem.

note: inferenceAgent with viz output — no extractable groundtruth

prompt: Stochastic utility-maximizing agent: sample an action from actionPrior and condition on achieving maximal expected utility. Agent chooses among 3 restaurants with noisy utilities. Return the distribution over the agent's action.
selection_rationale: Expected utility agent via factor: action probability proportional to exponentiated net utility.

prompt: Soft-max agent choosing food: uniform prior over ['bagel','cookie','doughnut']. factor() on the utility function (bagel:1, cookie:2, doughnut:3). Return the distribution over the agent's food choice (softMaxAgent).
selection_rationale: Soft-max action selection via factor(): actions sampled proportionally to exponentiated utility.

note: meaningPrior with ... placeholder ellipsis — not runnable

prompt: Literal listener RSA: 3 objects with features, 3 utterances with meanings. A literal listener conditions on the utterance being true of the sampled object. Return the distribution over objects given utterance 'blue'.
selection_rationale: Literal listener L0: uniform prior over objects, condition on utterance meaning — RSA base case.

prompt: Full RSA model: literalListener L0 (condition on meaning), speaker S1 (factor on L0 score of target), pragmaticListener L1 (observe from S1). Return L1's distribution over objects given utterance 'blue'.
selection_rationale: Full RSA model L1: pragmatic listener inverts speaker who inverts literal listener for implicature.

selection_rationale: Demonstrates the full Dirichlet-Categorical pattern used throughout hierarchical models chapters.

sequenceCondDist with viz — demonstrates i.i.d. independence, no inference

~~~~norun de Finetti template with undefined latentPrior/observe

prompt: Polya urn model: start with ['b','w'], draw a random ball, return it plus one more of the same color, repeat for 3 total samples. Infer the distribution over the resulting 3-ball sequence as a string.
selection_rationale: Polya urn demonstrates rich-get-richer dynamics and exchangeability; foundation for Dirichlet process.

prompt: de Finetti representation of the Polya urn: sample a latent weight from Beta(1,1) (for ['b','w'] urn), then generate 3 i.i.d. samples. Infer the distribution over the resulting 3-ball sequence string.
selection_rationale: de Finetti representation: exchangeable urn process has identical distribution when parameterized by latent Beta.

prompt: Simple Boolean Markov chain: each observation depends on the previous — flip(0.9) if prevObs was true, flip(0.1) otherwise. Generate a chain of length 10 starting from true.
selection_rationale: Markov chain generates persistent run-like sequences; illustrates temporal dependence vs i.i.d.

note: Markov word language model — generative only, no inference
Markov with Dirichlet transition priors — generative only

prompt: Subjective randomness model: given a sequence of 5 coin flips, infer whether it came from a fair (p=0.5) or biased (p=0.2) coin process. Return the posterior over isFair for sequences [F,F,T,F,T] and [F,F,F,F,F].
selection_rationale: Subjective randomness: people judge randomness by P(fair process|sequence), not P(sequence|fair process).

prompt: Markov-based subjective randomness: fair coin uses p=0.5 per step, trick coin has Markov dependence (stays same with p=0.9). Given sequence [F,F,T,F,T] vs [F,T,F,T,F], infer which is more likely from a fair process.
selection_rationale: Markov-based randomness model detects alternating sequences as non-random via Bayesian inference.

note: HMM generative model with Dirichlet priors — generative only, no inference

prompt: Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar (PCFG): implement S→NP VP, NP→D N, VP→V AP|V NP with uniform draws. Generate a sample sentence by calling S().
selection_rationale: PCFG as probabilistic program: non-terminals are procedures, constituent structure emerges from call graph.

note: ~~~~norun VP rule definition in PCFG — not self-contained
